In this connection it is to be noted that the Technical Advisory Board concluded that a homogeneous reactor has outstanding advantages for nuclear aircraft propulsion (although contingent upon the containment of hydroxides at high-around $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ -temperatures).

The mutual advantages of these two reactor systems are (1) separation of the heat exchange function from the reactor core, (2) a simple, rugged core structure, (3) self-stabilization associated with a liquid fuel, (4) simplified fuel removal and reprocessing, and (5) an arrangement whereby the highest system temperature occurs in the working fluid.

The established requirements<sup>1,2</sup> of a high-performance reactor for supersonic aircraft propulsion are high operating temperature---as required by the thermodynamics of aircraft engines, and high power density---as required to minimize the size of the shielded volume.
These are (1) inherent self-stabilization of the reactor against random transients and outright power change, and (2) elimination of the heat exchanger from the active lattice of the reactor.

(A solution of $\mathrm{UO}_3$ in a hydroxide now appears to be the most promising homogeneous fuel, but the hydroxides cannot be contained at the required temperatures without excessive corrosion.) On the other hand, molten fluoride salt mixtures containing the uranium salt have not only been contained but also circulated, pumped, and otherwise manipulated for long periods of time at temperatures of $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Ellis, "The Technical Problems of Aircraft Reactors," ANP-64, Parts I and II, June 1, 1951 and Jan.
The reactors are designed with a fuel exit temperature of $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and should permit very high power densities.
Thus, the absolute values of the results shown in Figure 1 are not exact, although the important parametric relationships are applicable to any circulating-fuel system.

Since from 60 to $80\%$ of the liquid fuel is in the heat exchanger, pumps, or piping, the minimum uranium investment in the overall system is obtained with a more dilute fuel solution than that for minimum critical mass.

The experimental reactor is, as nearly as is practical, a prototype of 350-megawatt, BeO-moderated, circulating-fuel aircraft reactor.
The core is approximately a 3-ft-square beryllium oxide cylinder in an Inconel shell.
While in the core, the fission processes raise the temperature of the fuel by several hundred degrees to $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
However, it is planned to limit the operational life of the reactor to about 1000 hours, but with as much time at design power of 3 megawatts as possible.

For example, the maximum temperature in each is $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and the temperature drop across the ARE is $350^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , while that in the aircraft reactor would be around $500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Thus, insofar as possible, the experimental reactor is a low-power 3-megawatt prototype of the 350-megawatt circulating-fuel reactor proposed for supersonic aircraft propulsion.
The design data for this reactor are given in Table 7.

text: Safety-three boron rods pass through the inner portion of the core with approximately $5.0\% \Delta \mathrm{k} / \mathrm{k}$ per rod.
Elevation and plan sections of this reactor are shown in Figures 9 and 10, respectively.
The innermost region of the lattice is the core, which is a cylinder approximately 3 ft in dia and 3 ft long.
The core is divided into six 60 deg sectors, each of which includes one serpentine fuel tube coil that passes through 11 stacks in series, as illustrated.

section: Figure Iq. Plan Section Of The Experimental Reactor
text: A reflector with a nominal thickness of 7.5 in. is located between the pressure shell and the cylindrical surface of the core.

section: Figure Iq. Plan Section Of The Experimental Reactor
text: The reflector consists of beryllium oxide blocks similar to the moderator blocks but with 0.5 in. holes for the passage of the reflector coolant.
The transition between laminar flow and turbulent flow occurs in the Reynolds number range 2000 to 5000.
Maintaining turbulent flow is so important that it was thought best to design for a Reynolds number of 10,000 and thereby allow for some deviation in flow rate or fluid physical properties.

This being the case, and since the design problems involved in the use of many series passes appeared to be similar to those associated with the use of a lesser number of series passes, it was decided to employ 11 series passes and avoid compromising the volumetric flow rate of the core fuel volume.
An arrangement consisting of six long tubes, each bent so as to constitute 11 series passes, appears to offer several important advantages.
The six bent tubes require a total of 12 end connections to manifolds outside the pressure shell.

The use of a liquid circuit within the pressure shell, other than the fuel circuit, is desired (1) to maintain the pressure shell essentially isothermal at a temperature of approximately $1150^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , (2) to cool the reflector with a fluid other than circulating fuel, and (3) to fill moderator interstices and other internal voids with a liquid maintained at a pressure higher than the fuel pressure to prevent a fuel tube leak from adding reactive material to the core.
The fluoride mixture NaF-KF-ZrF₄ (mole % 36-18-46), i.e., the fuel carrier without the UF₄ is well suited for this purpose.
The ARE fluid circuit is intended to handle toxic and corrosive fluorides at $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , and requires a considerable amount of design and developmental effort.
The locations of the major components of the system, including the reactor pits, heat exchanger room, reactor, and two heat disposal loops are illustrated in Figure 12.
The fuel then divides into two completely independent loops, each capable of dissipating $1500\mathrm{kw}$ .
Within each loop the fuel divides into two parallel $750\mathrm{kw}$ fuel-to-helium heat exchangers, passes through a centrifugal pump, and then to a common return trunk.
A line diagram of the primary cooling circuit is shown in Figure 13.
In an individual loop, helium flows from a centrifugal blower through a fuel-to-helium exchanger in which its temperature is increased from 250 to $750^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
After the helium is cooled to $250^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ in a helium-to-water exchanger, it passes through another heating and cooling cycle and is returned to the blower.
The reflector and pressure shell cooling system, as in the case of the fuel system, is divided into two heat disposal loops, each capable of handling the load associated with 1500 kw reactor power.
A line diagram of the secondary cooling circuit is shown in Figure 14.
A pump of this type, designed specifically for the ARE, will provide flow of 41 gpm with a 40 ft head (Figure 15).

It is possible that a centrifugal pump employing a frozen fluoride seal-a technique in which fluorides are frozen about the pump shaft effecting a seal-may be adapted; however, a gas seal pump has been satisfactorily operated at temperatures above $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ for several hundred hours.
The pumps will be constructed of type 316 stainless steel except that those parts in contact with the fluid stream will be of Inconel.
The heat ex-changers are of the cross-flow type with the fuel flowing through 1 in.OD, 0.109 in. wall Inconel tubing.
The fuel inlet and outlet headers are constructed of 4 in. pipe to which the five tube banks are welded.

The relatively high melting point of the circulating fuel and the secondary coolant (around $500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for the NaF-KF-ZrF $_4$ -UF $_4$ fuel with a composition in mole $\%$ of 34.7-17.4-44.4-3.5) requires that all equipment within which these coolants would be circulated be heated to permit loading, unloading, and also the possibility of low power operation.
The insulation heat-leakage at operating temperature has been calculated to be approximately 15 kw, and the heat capacity of the assembly has been estimated at 4000 Btu/°F.
With a constant heat input of 14 kw in addition to the insulation losses, it is estimated that the assembly can be heated from room temperature to 1200°F in approximately four days.
These heat exchangers have a total of approximately $40\mathrm{ft}^2$ of exposed freeflow area (both upstream and downstream) that, if allowed to radiate to ad-
jacent cold structure, would dissipate approximately 40 kw with the tubes at $1325^{\circ}\mathbf{F}$ .

However, the two basic characteristics of the circulating-fuel reactor are: (1) the reactor operating temperature is independent of the external pumps and heat exchanger; and (2) the power extracted is independent of reactor control rod motion.
The reactor will be made critical only after the fuel-carrying liquid has been brought up to a temperature of approximately $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
After the system has been checked for leaks and found to be tight, a fuel enrichment system will be used to bring the reactor to critical at the temperature of $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
This fuel-injection operation is accomplished by means of a mechanism shown in Figure 16.

To supplement the negative temperature coefficient, in case it is of insufficient magnitude, a single absorber rod having a total value of approximately $0.75\% \Delta k / k$ is provided in the center of the reactor.
The absorber rod is located in a 3 in dia hole that runs through the pressure shell and core in the center of the lattice.
By means of a direct drive, a 2 in dia boron absorber rod is inserted into this hole.
One of the motors is a 60-cycle reversible motor of constant speed that is manually actuated by a zero-position, momentary-contact, reversing switch located on the operating console.
Three safety rods for the ARE are located on 120 deg points at a radius of 7.5 in. from the center of the reactor.
The rods insert into 3 in. sleeves that pass through the reactor shell and core vertically from top to bottom.
- The driving mechanism is comprised of a reversible 60-cycle motor connected to a drive screw that is arranged so as to drive an electromagnet in a vertical travel of 36 inches.
Details of this actuator system and the safety rod are also shown in Figure 17 and 18.
A linediagram of the scram circuits with the actuating signals and interlocks is given in Figure 19.
The magnet circuits are expected to have a release time no shorter than 0.1 sec, which will allow much more positive operation of the system so far as spurious scams are concerned.
Each of the three safety rods has approximately $5\% \Delta k / k$ negative reactivity when inserted in the reactor.
Thus a total of approximately $15\%$ is available to shut down the reactor.

text: Similarly, the building crane and certain other items need not be operative during any short period in which operation from battery power is required, and these components are supplied only by the 440-v a-c system.
The various stations and measurements desired around the ARE fluids circuits are shown in Figure 21.
Development has now progressed to the point where levels can be controlled in high-temperature fluorides with great reliability for extended periods of more than 2000 hours.
The highest temperature rating of any commercial instrument known is $450^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Temperatures to $2000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ are measured by means of Chromel-Alumel thermocouples and recorded on multipoint Brown temperature recorders.

Two methods of thermocouple attachment are used at present: (1) pulse welding each thermocouple wire to the outside surface of pipes or containers, and (2) forming a beaded junction of the two thermocouple wires.
face of the pipe have revealed that there is no more than a 15 deg temperature drop from the internal thermocouple to that placed on the outside.
Thermocouples made by beading the wires are accurate within $\pm 1^{\circ}\mathbf{F}$ .
The Brown temperature recorders, with careful adjustment, will record temperatures accurately within $\pm 1^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ over the full range from 0 to $2000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
They do, however, show a tendency to drift after extended periods of operation and cause errors of the order of $5^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The static calculations are based on the multigroup method, as described in references 10 and 11.

The second calculation used a core of the composition of actual core, a core radius equal to $\sqrt{\mathrm{h}^2 + \frac{1}{2}\mathrm{R}^2}$ , (where $2\mathrm{h}$ is the height of the actual cylinder core and $\mathbf{R}$ is its radius), and a reflector corresponding in composition and thickness to the end reflector.
That is, the points on the curve are multiplied by $2\pi$ and the radius $r$ .

The result is integrated over $r$ , and this integral is divided by the cross-sectional area of the core to get the average over the cross-section; this average is then multiplied by $2 / \pi$ to get the average in the axial direction under the assumed axial cosine distribution.
The design data of the reactor are given in Table 7, pp.
For the purpose of the reactor calculation, the total volume of the reactor was broken down into five regions which are listed in Table 8, together with their compositions.

At full power, 3 megawatts, the total flux in the center of the reactor is $17 \times 10^{13}$ neutrons/sec. $\mathrm{cm}^2$ .
The thermal flux is $3.0 \times 10^{13}$ neutrons/sec. $\mathrm{cm}^2$ , and the fast flux is $14 \times 10^{13}$ neutrons/sec. $\mathrm{cm}^2$ .
The non-thermal flux averaged over the whole core is $7 \times 10^{13}$ neutrons/sec. $\mathrm{cm}^2$ and the likewise averaged thermal flux $1.5 \times 10^{13}$ neutrons/sec. $\mathrm{cm}^2$ .

However, if the control rods lagged behind the reactivity change to the extent that $\Delta \mathbf{k}_{\mathrm{eff}}$ remained uncompensated, a temperature increase of $\Delta \mathbf{k}_{\mathrm{eff}} / (10^{-4} / ^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ would result.
For $\Delta \mathbf{k}_{\mathrm{eff}} = 1\%$ , this amounts to $100^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Fast power oscillations would have a period given by $2\pi \sqrt{\lambda_0aSP}$ 12 where
$\lambda_{0}$ is the reciprocal of the mean neutron lifetime, about $10^{+4} \sec^{-1}$ , $\mathbf{a}$ the temperature coefficient of reactivity about $10^{-4} \mathsf{OC}$

This yields a period of $1 - 1/2$ sec, long enough for a servo system to keep up with the oscillation.
Furthermore, at 4 ft/sec it takes the fuel about 8 sec to pass through the eleven tubes, each 3 ft. long.

Hence, about $\frac{3}{4}$ of the delayed neutrons are given off inside the reactor and the loss of delayed neutrons due to fuel circulation does not appreciably decrease the damping which the delayed neutrons afford.
The damping effect due to the circulation of the fuel is illustrated by Figure 27 taken from Y-F10-109, "Note on the Non-Linear Kinetics of Circulating-Fuel Reactor," by S.

The three safety rods each effect a net change in reactivity of 0.053.

Estimated reactivity changes, $\Delta k / k_{\mathrm{eff}}$ , from room temperature to $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and to an assumed controlled reactor temperature of $1283^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ are given in Table 12.
The effect of xenon on reactivity at full power is to reduce the $\mathbf{k}_{\mathrm{eff}}$ of the clean reactor by $\Delta \mathbf{k} = 0.0031$ .
Maximum transient xenon reduces $\mathbf{k}_{\mathrm{eff}}$ further by $\Delta \mathbf{k} = 0.0082$ .

The temperature coefficient as a result of the xenon at full power is $\left(\frac{\Delta \mathbf{k} / \mathbf{k}}{\Delta \mathbf{T}}\right)_{\mathrm{Xe}} = +6.4 \times 10^{-7}$ per $^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ in the vicinity of $1400^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

Three safety rods are equally spaced on a 15-in. circle around the reactor axis.
The rods are 2-in. -dia cylinders of boron carbide with wall thickness of 0.335 in., and a beryllium oxide rod is attached to the end of each rod.

The net change in $\mathbf{k}_{\mathrm{eff}}$ for each rod is thus the sum of two reactivity contributions: (1) from the removal of beryllium oxide moderating material, and (2) from the insertion of a 2-in. -dia boron carbide neutron-absorbing rod.
Each boron carbide rod is worth -5.3% in $\Delta \mathbf{k} / \mathbf{k}$ , and each beryllium oxide rod in the same position is worth +0.16%.
The Inconel around the boron carbide rods is not an effective poison when the rods are inserted and results in a decrease in poison rod effect of 0.21%.
The net effect of insertion of the three safety rods is thus 15.75% in $\mathbf{k}_{\mathrm{eff}}$ .
The 15.75% in $\mathbf{k}_{\mathrm{eff}}$ corresponds to about 6 lb of uranium in the core.

Uranium trioxide seems to be soluble in a mixture of sodium and lithium hydroxides to an extent that would permit construction of a homogeneous high-temperature reactor if the low cross-section lithium isotope were available.[13] Lack of this isotope and a suitable container for the resulting liquid does not permit construction of a feasible reactor at present.
As the data in Table 13 indicates melting points sufficiently low for reactor operation may be obtained by dissolving UF $_4$ in any of several different systems.

section: Melting-Point Data For Potential Fuel Systems
text: The most promising fuel for the ARE seems to be obtained by addition of $\mathbf{U}\mathbf{F}_4$ to suitable compositions in the ternary system NaF-KF-ZrF $_4$ shown in Figure 29.

section: Melting-Point Data For Potential Fuel Systems
text: Mixtures obtained by addition of 5 mole % of $\mathbf{U}\mathbf{F}_4$ to a melt containing 46 mole % ZrF $_4$ , 36 mole % NaF, and 18 mole % KF melt in the range 465 to $495^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
FIGURE 29, THE SYSTEM NaF-KF-ZrF4 (TENTATIVE)
$500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ to give HF and $\mathrm{UO_2}$ .

It seems more reasonable, however, to mix the materials in a reasonably dry condition and accomplish the final drying and reconversion to fluoride of any oxide produced by hydrofluorination at 500 to $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The acid fluorides of the alkali metals are completely decomposed at $400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ so that complete removal of the excess HF would present no problem.
It seems apparent that the vapor pressure of the NaF-KF-ZrF $_4$ -UF $_4$ system will be low (probably less than 5 mm) at $1500^{\circ}\mathbf{F}$ .

The vapor pressure of $\mathrm{ZrF_4}$ is estimated in the open literature at $10^{-4}$ atmospheres at $9000\mathrm{K}$ and the vapor pressure of UF $_4$ is known to be about 6 mm at $1273^{\circ}\mathrm{K}$ .
The value of $3 \times 10^{-4}$ / $\mathrm{^o C}$ should be sufficient for control purposes.
The density for a fuel containing 18 to 20 lb of uranium tetrafluoride per cubic foot is about $3.0\mathrm{g} / \mathrm{cc}$ at $1500^{\mathrm{o}}\mathrm{F}$ .
The density of this fuel NaF-KF-ZrF $_4$ -UF $_4$ (mole % 34.7-17.4-44.4-3.5) is shown in Figure 31.

Examination of the literature has indicated that the viscosities of pure fused salts, including fluorides, should be 1 to 2 centipoises at temperatures 300 to $400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ above their melting points.

Preliminary experiments with the system NaF-KF- $\mathbf{U}\mathbf{F}_4$ at high uranium concentrations indicated that the viscosities were relatively high (2 1/2 to 4 centipoises at $800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ).
Experiments with various mixtures in the NaF-KF-ZrF₄-UF₄ system containing 2.0 mole % UF₄ have shown the viscosity to be less than 10 centipoises at 625°C; these data are shown in Figure 30.
Although further study will be necessary to establish accurate values for the actual fuel mixture (containing 3.5 mole % UF₄) it is evident that the viscosity values will be within acceptable limits.
FIGURE 30 VISCOSITY OF NaF-KF-ZrF4-UF4 AS A FUNCTION OF TEMPERATURE

This uranium would, presumably, be precipitated with ammonia or hydrogen peroxide, calcined to $\mathrm{U}_{3}\mathrm{O}_{8}$ or $\mathrm{UO}_3$ , reduced with hydrogen to $\mathrm{UO}_2$ , and hydrofluorinated to $\mathrm{UF}_4$ .

Among the common high-temperature structural metals none appears clearly superior in strength, and several of them (Inconel, 400 and 300 series stainless steels) possess sufficient high-temperature strength and creep resistance to be suitable, especially if thick-walled tubing is used.

Many structural metals, including nickel, Inconel, and the 300 and 400 series stainless steels, have been shown to be adequate containers for the fluoride mixtures under static, isothermal conditions.

In 500 hr of exposure, with the heated portion of a convection loop at 1500 and $250^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ temperature gradient, some 3 to 7 mils of pitting has been observed on an Inconel test loop containing carefully prepared NaF-KF-ZrF $_4$ -UF $_4$ mixture.
The ARE Inconel fuel tubes with 60 mil walls should certainly be adequate for the scheduled 1000 hr of reactor operation; meanwhile, it is believed that this corrosion can be materially reduced.

The metals which proved to have adequate resistance to static corrosion by mixtures of alkali, alkaline earth, zirconium, and uranium fluorides are Inconel, nickel, molybdenum, and the stainless steels of the 300 series.

The corrosion observed by metallographic examination of the capsule wall generally consisted of intergranular penetration of the metal and attack at the grain boundaries to the extent of 2 mils or less.
In these static tests Inconel seemed to be slightly more resistant to attack than did the 300 series stainless steels; the 400 series stainless steels appeared somewhat less satisfactory.

The data in Table 14 indicate that at $1500^{\circ}\mathbf{F}$ , and for intervals of 100 hr, it is possible to contain the fluorides in Inconel or type 316 stainless steel with little corrosive action.

The loops for these tests have been prepared by heliarc welding of $1/2$ in. -IPS schedule 40-pipe of the desired material.
The hot leg of the loop is maintained at $1500^{\circ}\mathbf{F}$ by electrical resistance heaters.
Temperatures in the cold leg of the loops are maintained at 150 to $180^{\circ}\mathbf{F}$ below those of the hot leg.
Under these conditions of operation the circulation rate is 6 to 8 ft/min.
In initial tests using mixtures of $\mathbf{U}\mathbf{F}_4$ with alkali fluorides only loops of Inconel and nickel have operated for 500 hr at temperature without difficulty.
Loops of series 400 stainless steel were plugged by the deposition of high-melting-point material in the cold leg after less than 30 hours.
Loops of 300 series stainless steel showed similar but less rapid plugging, and circulation was stopped by this phenomenon after 100 to 150 hours.

Tests of 500 hr duration in loops of stainless steel have indicated virtually negligible corrosion; photomicrographs of hot-leg sections of loops exposed to NaF-KF-LiF mixture with and without added NaK are shown in Figure 32.
Recent tests in which solutions of $\mathbf{U}\mathbf{F}_4$ in NaF-KF-ZrF $_4$ mixtures have been exposed for 500 hr in Inconel yielded considerably more favorable results.
The hot legs of the loops showed scattered pitting to a depth of 3 to 4 mils with infrequent intergranular attack to a depth of 7 mils (see Figure 33).
A thin (perhaps 1 mil) nonmetallic layer was observed in the

In any case, it appears that for the ARE the corrosion that must at present be considered normal in Inconel for periods up to 1000 hr would not be prohibitive.
Present designs for the reactor call for 60-mil tube walls in the hot zone.
The annular cans are fabricated of type-316 stainless steel and have an inner diameter of 1 l/4 in. and an outer diameter of 2 inches.
The cans are 1.95 in. long, and about 18 cans will be required per control rod assembly.
The B $_4$ C-Fe slugs contain approximately 20% by volume iron, which acts as a binder.
The B $_4$ C slugs are 1.86 O.D. by 1.26 I.D. by 1.895 in. long.
At this time 20 of the $\mathbf{B}_4\mathbf{C}$ slugs have been successfully hot pressed.
The slugs were fabricated by hot pressing at $1520^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ under a pressure of 2500 psi using graphite dies.
The regulating rod will be made by using slugs of $\mathrm{Al}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3}$ impregnated with $\mathbf{B}_4\mathbf{C}$ contained in one of the 300 series stainless steels.

Rods of two strengths, in which the only difference will be the amount of $\mathbf{B}_4\mathbf{C}$ added to $\mathrm{Al}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3}$ , will be made for evaluation in the hot critical experiment.

In one case 0.022 to $0.024\mathrm{g / cc}$ of $\mathbf{B}_4\mathbf{C}$ will be added to $\mathrm{Al}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3}$ , and the other rod will contain 0.005 to $0.006\mathrm{g / cc}$ of $\mathbf{B}_4\mathbf{C}$ with $\mathrm{Al}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3}$ .
The $\mathrm{Al}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3}$ slugs will have the same dimensions as the $\mathbf{B}_4\mathbf{C}$ slugs except that they will be longer.
The dilute slugs will be 2.14 in. long and the strong slugs will be 2 in. long.
The slugs for the regulating rod being made are hot pressed at $1675^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ in graphite dies under pressure of 2500 psi.

Metallographic examination of a slug of $\mathrm{Al}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3}$ and $\mathbf{B}_4\mathbf{C}$ has shown that the two materials are quite compatible at the operating temperature of the control rods and that there is no undue reaction between this mixture and the 300 series stainless steel container materials.

Some of the $\mathbf{B}_4\mathbf{C}$ -Fe slugs have been canned in 300 series stainless steel and brazed through the Microbrazing cycle, which consists of heating at $1130^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for $10\mathrm{min}$ ; they
were then held at $815^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for 100 hr with slight reaction.

Two types of physical tests have been conducted: (1) tube burst tests to furnish hoop stress data for metals in contact with the fluoride medium and (2) stress-rupture tests in which the specimen is in a fluoride environment.
The sample is held in a bath of fluoride maintained at $816^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The hoop stress is produced by placing an inert gas atmosphere inside the tube while the outside of the tube is bathed in the fluorides at $816^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Table 16 is a summary of the stress-rupture data being obtained on Inconel specimens in the fluoride salt, NaF-KF-LiF-UF $_4$ (mole % 10.9-43.5-44.5-1.1), at $816^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

In addition to the tests listed in the table, one Inconel sample has been under a stress of 4000 psi for 350 hr and another has withstood 5000 psi for 525 hr without undue elongation.
Furthermore, Inconel tube burst tests have successfully operated from 500 to 1000 hr at stresses of 2000 to 3000 psi without severe corrosion being induced by the stress.
Inconel capsules containing several different fluoride mixtures have been irradiated in both the LITR and the X-10 graphite pile.

Nevertheless, the irradiation of other fluoride mixtures at power levels in the range that will exist in the ARE (approximately 100 watts/cc) have shown no apparent increase in decomposition or corrosion owing to the radiation field.
These data are summarized in Table 17 for several irradiations.

As indicated in Table 17, exposures of samples of the NaF-KF-UF $_4$ fuel in Inconel at the maximum flux of the X-10 graphite pile caused a power dissipation of approximately 65 watts/cc of material and have been quite satisfactory.
The irradiated samples showed no detectable gas evolution because of the irradiation, nor--after exposures up to 400 hr--was there any positive evidence of damage.

The corrosion of the capsule, as measured by metallographic techniques, was the same order of magnitude of that in similar tests conducted outside the radiationfield (Figure 34), as was the chemical analysis for structural elements in the solidified liquid.

Exposure of similar samples in the LITR at $800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ resulted in power dissipations of 800 watts/cc of material but yielded results that may be interpreted as considerably increased corrosion under the strong irradiation.

text: In only one of five exposures (Table 17) was there any indication of damage beyond that experienced in the control specimen.

A preliminary experimental study of the nuclear design of the experimental reactor is to be made during the summer of 1952 at ORNL by building some of the major components into a system that will be made critical with enriched uranium.

The experiments will be done at a power level of the order of 1 watt, maximum, at room temperature and will yield information on uranium requirements, control problems, and power and neutron-flux distributions.
It is expected that assembly of the mockup will start about June 1 and that the system will be made critical about July 15.
The materials will be packed as a dry powder in stainless steel tubes, 1 l/4 in. in dia and 40 in. long, that can be sealed to minimize moisture pick-up.
The beryllium oxide blocks will be stacked, with axes vertical, in a right cylinder, 36 in. high and 47 in. in dia; also with its axis vertical.
Of the 500-odd total hours available for operation of the system after the original loading with enriched fuel, probably not more than 100 hours will be available for power operation.

Air or other gas will be held at a pressure of approximately 75 psi at room temperature and all welds and joints of any kind will be gone over with a bubble solution for the purpose of locating leaks.
After both circuits are tested pneumatically, helium at 75 psi will be substituted for the gas in the primary circuit and a vacuum will be pulled on the secondary circuit.
While maintaining temperatures at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ both circuits will be evacuated by pumping with vapor traps ahead of the pumps.
The objective will be to go critical at a temperature of $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ with the shim rods as nearly completely withdrawn as the controlled loading will permit.
The first attempt to make these measurements will be done when the reactor has reached a value of $k = \sim 0.98$ or at a source multiplication of about 50.
To do this the fuel temperature will be lowered from the equilibrium value of $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ to approximately $1175^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ when the source multiplication is approximately 50.
It is believed that a first measurement can be made when the reactivity is approximately 0.98 with higher accuracy results obtainable as $k$ is increased toward 1.00.

After the rod calibration has been made to the best degree warranted the reactor will be raised in temperature by the external heating facilities to a temperature of some $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ since this is the mean temperature level for all future operation.

This will provide of the order of $2\%$ in k available in shim rods to take care of poison as the reactor operates so that no further fuel addition should be necessary after the critical loading at $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ has been accomplished.

The mean fuel temperature of $1300^{\circ}\mathbf{F}$ will be maintained quite closely, but the fuel inlet temperature will drop and the reactor outlet temperature will rise until at full power there will be a temperature rise across the reactor of some $350^{\circ}\mathbf{F}$ .
\mathbf {E} = \mathrm {a} _ {\mathrm {i}} \boldsymbol {\Phi} + \mathrm {a} _ {2} \Theta_ {\mathrm {i}} + \mathrm {a} _ {3} \Theta_ {\mathrm {o}} + \mathrm {a} _ {4}
\Theta_ {0} = \text {r e a c t o r o u t l e t t e m p e r a t u r e}
It will be possible to determine the maximum $\Delta k / k$ step for which the system is stable and permits no more than $10\%$ overshoot of mean temperature.
The ARE building is a mill type of structure designed to house the ARE and the necessary facilities for its operation (Figure 35).
The building has a full basement $80 \times 105$ ft, a crane bay $42 \times 105$ ft, and a one-story service wing $38 \times 105$ feet.
The building consists of a full basement $80 \times 105$ feet.
The crane is a floor-operated 10-ton bridge crane having a maximum lift of 25 ft above the main floor level.
Plan and elevation drawings of the building are shown in Figures 36 and 37.
Removable blocks within the test pit are shown in Figure 37.
The electrical supply to the building consists of an extension of the plant area 13.2-kv line.
Both 440 and 110 to 208-v distribution are made within the building.
A 100,000-gal earth settling pond is provided approximately 1500 ft south of the building and connected to the test pits by a buried steel pipeline.

It is felt that sufficient basic work has been done to establish the possibility of building a circulating-fuel reactor for operation at a maximum fuel temperature of approximately $1500^{\mathrm{O}}\mathrm{F}$ for a significant number of hours.
